---
title: "Why *The Selfish Gene* still matters"
description: "Fifty years on, what the book actually argued — and what it didn't."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [books, biology]
draft: true
---

Most people who think they have an opinion about *The Selfish Gene* have an opinion about its title. Dawkins published the book in 1976, and the bumper-sticker version — "we are all selfish because our genes are selfish" — has been repeated, misquoted, and railed against ever since.

The actual argument is more interesting. Genes don't *want* anything. The book asks a different question: if natural selection retains or discards heritable units, what are those units? Dawkins's answer is that the gene is the smallest thing for which the answer is unambiguous. An organism dies; a gene gets copied, sometimes for billions of years. The body is the vehicle; the gene is the replicator.

That reframing buys you a lot. Hamilton's rule — *altruism evolves when rB > C* — falls out of it naturally. So does kin selection in social insects, the evolution of cooperation in repeated games, and the persistence of traits like sickle-cell anaemia that look harmful at the level of the individual. The book is the readable translation of twenty years of mathematical work by Hamilton, Williams, Maynard Smith, and Trivers.

What's aged? The framework has competition now — multi-level selection, niche construction, evo-devo, transgenerational epigenetics. Dawkins's framing turns out to be one good lens, not the only one. But it remains the lens you reach for when asking *why does this gene persist?*, and the alternatives mostly extend the gene's-eye view rather than replace it.

The other reason the book matters: it taught a generation of biologists to think about heredity as *information* rather than matter. For anyone arriving at biology from a software background, that move is already familiar — what gets copied is the schema, not the substrate. Dawkins made it in 1976, before the genome was sequenced.

If you've never read it, do. If you read it long ago and remember the bumper-sticker version, read it again.
